# Accelerometer Introduction

Accelerometry turns continuous movement signals into information about physical activity, sedentary behavior, and sleep-wake patterns. This module gives the conceptual foundation for the workflow taught in the rest of the course: what an accelerometer records, what it can estimate, why placement matters, and how a study moves from a device on a participant to a clean analysis dataset.

<div class="learning-objectives">
<strong>By the end of this module, you should be able to:</strong>

1. Explain what an accelerometer records and how raw signals become research outcomes.
2. Distinguish common accelerometer-derived physical activity, sedentary-behavior, body-posture, activity-type, and sleep-wake measures.
3. Describe how device placement and protocol choices shape the data that can be collected and interpreted.
4. Read placement and device tables as planning tools rather than universal rules.
</div>

## Lesson 1.1: What is an accelerometer?

An accelerometer is a sensor that records acceleration over time, commonly along three axes. In human movement research, the device does not directly “know” whether a person is exercising, sitting, or asleep. Researchers combine the recorded signal with a documented processing method, a wear location, and a study protocol to derive interpretable outcomes.

The raw signal is commonly stored in gravitational units (*g*) or metres per second squared (*m/s²*). Later in the pipeline, the signal can be summarized into metrics such as **ENMO** (*Euclidean Norm Minus One*), a raw-acceleration metric that subtracts 1 *g* to remove the static effect of gravity and summarize dynamic movement. Intensity summaries such as **MVPA** (*moderate-to-vigorous physical activity*) are not direct sensor readings; they are derived after applying thresholds or algorithms to processed data.

### From device to dataset

The complete pipeline has many decisions, but the big picture is simple:

<div class="workflow-flow" aria-label="Accelerometer workflow from device to dataset">
<div class="workflow-step"><span>1</span><strong>Plan and deploy</strong><p>Choose device, placement, sampling, wear protocol, and participant instructions.</p></div>
<div class="workflow-step"><span>2</span><strong>Download</strong><p>Retrieve raw files and document return status, dates, and operator decisions.</p></div>
<div class="workflow-step"><span>3</span><strong>Organize and convert</strong><p>Use reproducible folders, IDs, logs, and only convert formats when the pipeline needs it.</p></div>
<div class="workflow-step"><span>4</span><strong>Process</strong><p>Run GGIR or another approved workflow to calibrate, detect non-wear, and summarize movement.</p></div>
<div class="workflow-step"><span>5</span><strong>Inspect quality</strong><p>Review reports, plots, missingness, calibration error, clipping, and valid-day status.</p></div>
<div class="workflow-step"><span>6</span><strong>Clean and analyse</strong><p>Apply documented rules, derive outcomes, prepare the final Stata dataset, and report decisions.</p></div>
</div>

<div class="video-placeholder">
<strong>Video 1.1 — What is an accelerometer? (target: 5–7 minutes)</strong><br>
Use this lecture to explain the difference between a sensor signal, an epoch-level summary, and a research outcome.
</div>

### Optional learning activity

In two or three sentences, explain the difference between (a) a raw accelerometer signal, (b) a 5-second epoch file, and (c) an accelerometer-derived physical-activity outcome.

## Lesson 1.2: What can accelerometers measure?

Accelerometer recordings can support estimates of several dimensions of movement behavior. The exact outcome depends on the device, placement, algorithm, calibration, and processing choices. Common outcomes include physical-activity volume and intensity, sedentary time, body posture, activity type, and sleep-wake timing [Quante et al., 2015](references.html#ref-quante-2015-a); [Arvidsson et al., 2019](references.html#ref-arvidsson-2019).

| Domain | Examples of potential outcomes | Interpretation note |
|---|---|---|
| Physical activity | Movement volume, energy expenditure, steps, light activity, MVPA, vigorous activity | Estimates are method-dependent; report the processing approach and thresholds. |
| Sedentary behavior | Sedentary time, breaks, and accumulated sedentary bouts | Posture-sensitive estimates require an appropriate placement and algorithm. |
| Sleep-wake behavior | Sleep duration, onset, offset, midpoint, latency, efficiency, fragmentation | These are algorithm-derived estimates, not a clinical diagnosis. |
| Body posture | Sitting, standing, lying | Usually depends on placement such as thigh or trunk sensors. |
| Activity type | Walking, running, cycling, stair-stepping | Activity-type classification is more algorithm-specific than volume/intensity summaries. |

<figure class="course-table-figure">
<figcaption>Evidence details: examples of potential accelerometer-derived measures.</figcaption>
<div class="table-scroll">
<table class="course-table course-table--measure">
<thead>
<tr>
<th scope="col">Category</th>
<th scope="col">Indicators</th>
<th scope="col">Measurement</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<th scope="rowgroup" rowspan="8">Physical activity (<a href="references.html#ref-arvidsson-2019">Arvidsson et al., 2019</a>)</th>
<td>Energy expenditure</td>
<td>(kcal or METs)</td>
</tr>
<tr><td>Step count</td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td>sedentary time</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr><td>light-intensity PA</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr><td>moderate PA</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr><td>vigorous PA</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr><td>moderate-to-vigorous PA (MVPA)</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr><td>total activity counts per day</td><td></td></tr>
<tr>
<th scope="rowgroup" rowspan="8">Sleep-wake behaviors (<a href="references.html#ref-quante-2015-a">Quante et al., 2015</a>)</th>
<td>sleep duration</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr><td>nap duration</td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td>sleep latency</td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td>sleep maintenance efficiency</td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td>sleep fragmentation</td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td>sleep onset time</td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td>sleep offset time</td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td>sleep midpoint</td><td></td></tr>
<tr>
<th scope="rowgroup" rowspan="3">Body posture with thigh placement (<a href="references.html#ref-arvidsson-2019">Arvidsson et al., 2019</a>)</th>
<td>Sitting</td>
<td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td>
</tr>
<tr><td>standing</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr><td>lying</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr>
<th scope="rowgroup" rowspan="4">Activity type (<a href="references.html#ref-arvidsson-2019">Arvidsson et al., 2019</a>)</th>
<td>walking</td>
<td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td>
</tr>
<tr><td>running</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr><td>cycling</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
<tr><td>stair-stepping</td><td>Daily time (minutes, proportion (%))</td></tr>
</tbody>
</table>
</div>
</figure>

## Lesson 1.3: Placement and protocol decisions

Before a monitor is initialized, the study team must choose the device model, wear location, start and stop dates, instructions for participants, and criteria for evaluating whether a recording is usable. These decisions affect participant burden and the outcomes that can be validly interpreted.

Different monitor placements are useful for different questions. A thigh-worn monitor may support posture classification, while wrist placement often improves comfort and continuous wear. The best placement follows the research question and protocol rather than a universal rule [Migueles et al., 2017](references.html#ref-migueles-2017); [Kerr et al., 2017](references.html#ref-kerr-2017).

### Quick reference: placement planning

| Placement | Good fit when the study needs... | Main planning risk |
|---|---|---|
| Wrist | High compliance, 24-hour wear, broad activity/sleep workflows | Intensity estimates and cut-points must match wrist placement. |
| Hip/waist | Ambulatory activity volume and historical comparability | Lower comfort and removal during sleep/bathing may reduce wear time. |
| Thigh | Posture-sensitive outcomes such as sitting/standing/lying | Attachment method and skin comfort must be planned carefully. |
| Chest/trunk | Trunk acceleration and some gait/posture applications | More obtrusive; not the default for many free-living studies. |
| Ankle | Locomotion in infants/toddlers or populations where hip/wrist are limited | Sleep vs sedentary separation may be difficult. |
| Low back | Balance and some whole-body movement applications | Adherence and belt attachment need protocol attention. |

### Evidence details: placement strengths and limitations

<figure class="course-table-figure">
<figcaption>Evidence details: accelerometer placement options.</figcaption>
<div class="table-scroll">
<table class="course-table course-table--placement">
<thead>
<tr>
<th scope="col">Placement</th>
<th scope="col">Strengths</th>
<th scope="col">Limitations</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<th scope="row">Wrist (<a href="references.html#ref-doherty-2017">Doherty et al., 2017</a>)</th>
<td>Large-scale wrist-worn protocols have been used successfully in population assessment. Participants may wear wrist-worn devices for longer periods than hip-worn devices <a href="references.html#ref-kerr-2017">Kerr et al., 2017</a>.</td>
<td>Sometimes less accurate than hip placement for selected physical-activity estimates <a href="references.html#ref-kerr-2017">Kerr et al., 2017</a>.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">Chest (<a href="references.html#ref-nazarahari-2018">Nazarahari &amp; Rouhani, 2018</a>)</th>
<td>Chest-derived acceleration can reflect physical activity intensity and has been used to detect postures and walking modalities <a href="references.html#ref-mukaino-2022">Mukaino et al., 2022</a>; <a href="references.html#ref-nazarahari-2018">Nazarahari &amp; Rouhani, 2018</a>.</td>
<td>Trunk movement measurement with a chest-mounted accelerometer may be less easy to implement than wrist-worn protocols <a href="references.html#ref-mukaino-2022">Mukaino et al., 2022</a>.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">Low back (<a href="references.html#ref-williamson-2026">Williamson et al., 2026</a>)</th>
<td>Commonly used for balance assessment and whole-body movement applications <a href="references.html#ref-ghislieri-2019">Ghislieri et al., 2019</a>.</td>
<td>Participant adherence can be challenging in skin-taped or body-worn protocols <a href="references.html#ref-schneller-2017">Schneller et al., 2017</a>.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">Hip (<a href="references.html#ref-migueles-2017">Migueles et al., 2017</a>)</th>
<td>Hip placement has strong historical use and can reflect energy expenditure well in laboratory and locomotive activities <a href="references.html#ref-hildebrand-2014">Hildebrand et al., 2014</a>.</td>
<td>Hip placement may be perceived as less comfortable and more socially obtrusive than wrist placement, lowering wear-time compliance in some groups <a href="references.html#ref-scott-2017">Scott et al., 2017</a>.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">Thigh (<a href="references.html#ref-kozey-keadle-2011">Kozey-Keadle et al., 2011</a>)</th>
<td>Thigh inclination can classify sitting more accurately than pelvis inclination, and thigh-based algorithms have been developed for activities such as stair walking and cycling <a href="references.html#ref-peterson-2015">Peterson et al., 2015</a>; <a href="references.html#ref-skotte-2014">Skotte et al., 2014</a>.</td>
<td>Attachment may require tape solutions that can loosen with activity, clothing changes, or participant discomfort <a href="references.html#ref-arvidsson-2019">Arvidsson et al., 2019</a>.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">Ankle (<a href="references.html#ref-dorris-2025">Dorris et al., 2025</a>)</th>
<td>Can support continuous 24-hour collection and may capture locomotion when hip or wrist placement is limited <a href="references.html#ref-campbell-2021">Campbell et al., 2021</a>.</td>
<td>May have difficulty differentiating sedentary time from sleep in young children <a href="references.html#ref-hager-2016">Hager et al., 2016</a>.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
</div>
</figure>

<div class="review-note">
<strong>Protocol review required before recording:</strong> Confirm the wear schedule, sampling frequency, valid-day criteria, non-wear algorithm, reporting units, and thresholds with the study's principal investigator or data lead. Prior cohort protocols are examples, not defaults for every study.
</div>

### Device comparison

Device selection should follow the research question, protocol, planned wear duration, placement, sampling needs, and available analytical workflow. The table below is a quick planning reference; specifications should always be verified with the manufacturer's current documentation before procurement or initialization. A previous draft included a consumer-device row with an incorrect product name; that row has been removed rather than teaching an incorrect device label.

<figure class="course-table-figure">
<figcaption>Quick reference: illustrative comparison of selected accelerometer devices.</figcaption>
<div class="table-scroll">
<table class="course-table course-table--wide">
<thead>
<tr>
<th scope="col">Brand</th>
<th scope="col">Primary Accelerometer</th>
<th scope="col">Sample Rate</th>
<th scope="col">Data Storage</th>
<th scope="col">Dimensions</th>
<th scope="col">Gyroscope Range</th>
<th scope="col">Wear Location</th>
<th scope="col">Sensor Resolution</th>
<th scope="col">Water Resistance</th>
<th scope="col">Battery Life</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<th scope="row">ActiGraph</th>
<td>+/- 8G</td>
<td>30-100 Hertz</td>
<td>180 Days / 4GB</td>
<td>4.6 x 3.3 x 1.5 cm</td>
<td>Device/model dependent; verify before initialization</td>
<td>Wrist, waist, ankle, thigh</td>
<td>16 bit, accel and gyro</td>
<td>IPX7 (1 meter, 30 minutes)</td>
<td>25 days</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">Axivity</th>
<td>±2 / 4 / 8 / 16 g configurable</td>
<td>12.5 - 1600Hz configurable</td>
<td>1024Mb flash non-volatile</td>
<td>23 x 32.5 x 8.9 (mm)</td>
<td>±125/250/500/1000/2000 dps configurable</td>
<td>Wrist, thigh, waist, or other protocol-specific placement</td>
<td>Device/model dependent; verify before initialization</td>
<td>Device/model dependent; verify before deployment</td>
<td>7+ days @ 100Hz, Accel &amp; Gyro; 31+ days @ 50Hz, Accel only</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">GENEActiv</th>
<td>+/- 8g</td>
<td>Selectable 10-100Hz</td>
<td>0.5Gb non-volatile</td>
<td>43mm x 40mm x 13mm</td>
<td>Not used in the course example; verify if needed</td>
<td>Wrist or protocol-specific placement</td>
<td>12 bit (3.9mg)</td>
<td>10m (IP67 – 1m 24hrs)</td>
<td>30 days @20Hz, 7 days @100Hz</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">activPAL</th>
<td>± 4g</td>
<td>ΔT(°C), 20/50 Hz, 10/25 Hz</td>
<td>64 MB</td>
<td>26 × 43 × 5 mm</td>
<td>Not the main measurement channel for the course example</td>
<td>Thigh</td>
<td>xyz, 10 bit</td>
<td>IP65</td>
<td>7 days</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<th scope="row">Fibion SENS</th>
<td>-4G to 4G</td>
<td>12.5 Hz (optionally 25 Hz)</td>
<td>Up to 14 days</td>
<td>47 x 22 x 4.5 mm</td>
<td>Not specified in the course source; verify with manufacturer documentation</td>
<td>Body-worn placement according to the Fibion protocol and study question</td>
<td>Not specified in the course source; verify with manufacturer documentation</td>
<td>IP68 for 1.5 meters for up to 1 hour</td>
<td>150 days</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
</div>
</figure>

<div class="video-placeholder">
<strong>Video 1.2 — Choosing placement and a wear protocol (target: 7–9 minutes)</strong><br>
Use a narrated comparison table and photographs or diagrams of approved placements. Do not use participant-identifiable images without appropriate permission.
</div>

### Optional learning activity

For a fictional seven-day study of adults, write a one-paragraph protocol that states:

1. The proposed wear location and why it fits the research question.
2. Whether the monitor is worn continuously or only while awake.
3. What instructions participants receive if the device is removed.
4. Which study team member will review data quality after download.

## Wrap-up

You now have the conceptual map for the rest of the course. The next module moves from study design to hands-on fieldwork: programming devices, downloading recordings, and assessing whether data collection was complete.

## Knowledge check

1. What is the difference between a raw acceleration signal and an activity outcome?
2. Why does device placement matter when interpreting accelerometer data?
3. Name two study decisions that should be documented before devices are deployed.

<details>
<summary>Suggested answers</summary>

1. The raw signal is a time series recorded by the sensor; an outcome is an estimate derived after processing the signal with documented methods.
2. Placement changes the movement signal and determines which algorithms and interpretations are appropriate.
3. Examples include wear location, wear schedule, initialization dates, participant instructions, sampling settings, and valid-data criteria.

</details>
